patterns.name: Monolith
patterns.description: A single deployable unit containing all application logic, data access, and UI in one process. The original default architecture pattern, predating distributed systems. The key insight is that co-location of code eliminates network boundaries but couples deployment.
principles: Single deployable artifact
Shared memory space for all components
Unified data model and schema
Single build and release pipeline
In-process function calls between modules
when_to_use: Greenfield project with a small team (fewer than 8 developers)
Domain boundaries are not yet well understood
Low operational maturity — team lacks experience running distributed systems
Latency-sensitive operations that benefit from in-process calls
Rapid prototyping where speed of iteration matters more than scalability
avoid_when: Multiple teams need to deploy independently on different cadences
Parts of the system have fundamentally different scaling requirements
Regulatory or compliance requirements mandate isolation between subsystems
The codebase has grown beyond 500k LOC and build times exceed 20 minutes
signals: All business logic resides in a single repository with a single build output
Database schema is shared across all features with no schema boundaries
Deployment requires redeploying the entire application even for a one-line change
All modules share the same runtime process and memory space
Feature teams must coordinate releases because of shared deployment

patterns.name: Modular Monolith
patterns.description: A monolith deliberately structured into well-defined modules with explicit boundaries, enforced interfaces, and independent internal data stores. Popularized by Shopify and others as a pragmatic middle ground. The key insight is that modularity is an architectural property, not a deployment property.
principles: Explicit module boundaries enforced at compile time
Each module owns its data and exposes it only through defined interfaces
Modules communicate via well-defined contracts, not direct database access
Single deployment unit but logically separated domains
Module dependency graph is acyclic and enforced by tooling
when_to_use: Team wants the simplicity of a monolith with clear domain separation
Preparing for a potential future migration to microservices
Domain boundaries are known but operational overhead of distribution is not justified
Organization has fewer than 20 developers working on the same product
Need strong consistency guarantees that are easier in-process
avoid_when: Teams need independent deployment and release cycles today
Modules have fundamentally different runtime requirements (e.g., GPU vs CPU)
Organization has more than 50 developers and coordination cost is already high
Different modules need different technology stacks
signals: Codebase has clearly defined module directories with enforced import restrictions
Each module has its own database schema or schema namespace
Inter-module communication goes through defined API surfaces, not shared tables
Build system enforces module dependency rules and rejects circular dependencies
Modules can be tested independently with their own test suites

patterns.name: Microservices
patterns.description: An architecture where the application is composed of small, independently deployable services, each owning its own data and running in its own process. Formalized by James Lewis and Martin Fowler in 2014. The key insight is that organizational autonomy and independent deployability are the primary drivers, not service size.
principles: Each service is independently deployable and replaceable
Services own their data — no shared databases
Smart endpoints and dumb pipes
Decentralized governance and polyglot persistence
Design for failure with circuit breakers and bulkheads
Each service is organized around a business capability
when_to_use: Multiple autonomous teams need to deploy independently on different cadences
Different parts of the system have fundamentally different scaling profiles
Organization has mature DevOps practices including CI/CD, monitoring, and container orchestration
Business domains are well understood and stable enough to define service boundaries
Need to adopt different technology stacks for different problem domains
avoid_when: Team lacks operational maturity for distributed systems debugging
Domain boundaries are not yet understood — premature decomposition creates distributed monoliths
Organization has fewer than 15 developers total
Strong consistency across services is a hard requirement
Network latency between services is unacceptable for the use case
signals: Independent teams deploying different business domains on different release cycles
Each service has its own repository, CI pipeline, and data store
Services communicate over network protocols (HTTP, gRPC, messaging)
Failure in one service does not cascade to bring down the entire system
Different services use different technology stacks suited to their problem domain
Service discovery and API gateway infrastructure is in place

patterns.name: Mini Services
patterns.description: A pragmatic middle ground between monolith and microservices where services are larger than microservices, typically encompassing a subdomain rather than a single bounded context. Emerged as teams realized many microservice deployments were over-decomposed. The key insight is that service granularity should match team structure and deployment needs, not an arbitrary size metric.
principles: Services align to subdomains containing multiple bounded contexts
Fewer services reduce operational overhead while maintaining deployment independence
Shared libraries within a service are acceptable
Team owns one or two services rather than a dozen
Pragmatic data sharing within a service boundary is allowed
when_to_use: Organization wants independent deployment but not the overhead of hundreds of microservices
Teams are structured around subdomains rather than individual bounded contexts
Operational maturity is moderate — can handle some distributed systems but not extreme fragmentation
The system has 10-30 natural service boundaries based on business capability mapping
avoid_when: Teams need fully independent technology stacks per bounded context
Individual bounded contexts have vastly different scaling requirements
Organization has the operational maturity and team count to support true microservices
signals: Services contain multiple related bounded contexts within a single subdomain
Team count is 5-15 and each team owns 1-3 services
Services share a technology stack within the organization
Deployment units are coarser than bounded contexts but finer than the entire application
Internal complexity within a service is managed through modular code, not further decomposition

patterns.name: Nanoservices
patterns.description: Extremely fine-grained services where each service handles a single operation or function, often deployed as individual serverless functions. Generally considered an anti-pattern when taken to extremes. The key insight is that over-decomposition creates distributed complexity that exceeds the complexity it was meant to solve.
principles: One function per deployable unit
Stateless execution
Event-triggered invocation
Extreme isolation at the cost of coordination complexity
Each function has its own deployment lifecycle
when_to_use: Individual operations have wildly different invocation frequencies and scaling needs
Functions are truly independent with no coordination requirements
Cost optimization through pay-per-invocation pricing is a primary driver
Processing is embarrassingly parallel with no shared state
avoid_when: Operations require coordination or sequencing — orchestration overhead dominates
Cold start latency is unacceptable for the use case
Debugging distributed traces across dozens of functions is beyond team capability
The system requires transactional consistency across operations
Total cost of observability infrastructure exceeds compute savings
signals: Each deployable unit contains a single function or operation
Functions are triggered by events with no direct inter-function calls
System has hundreds of independently deployed function units
Tracing a single business transaction requires correlating logs across many function invocations
Cold start latency appears in production metrics

patterns.name: Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)
patterns.description: An architectural style where application components provide services to other components via a communications protocol over a network, typically mediated by an enterprise service bus (ESB). Formalized in the early 2000s by enterprise vendors. The key insight is reusability of business services across the enterprise, though the ESB often became a centralized bottleneck.
principles: Services are reusable business capabilities
Standardized service contracts (often WSDL/SOAP)
Service abstraction hides implementation details
Service composability enables building higher-order processes
Enterprise service bus mediates communication
Canonical data models for interoperability
when_to_use: Enterprise integration across multiple legacy systems and vendors
Need to expose business capabilities as reusable services across departments
Existing investment in ESB infrastructure and middleware
Regulatory requirements mandate centralized message auditing and transformation
avoid_when: Building a new greenfield application — microservices or modular monolith are simpler
Team is small and does not need enterprise-grade service governance
ESB becomes a bottleneck and single point of failure
Canonical data model creates coupling across all services
signals: Enterprise service bus sits between all service communications
Services expose WSDL or similar formal contract definitions
Canonical data model is enforced across service boundaries
Service registry and governance tooling is in place
Message transformation and routing happens in middleware, not endpoints

patterns.name: Hexagonal Architecture (Ports and Adapters)
patterns.description: An architecture where the application core is isolated from external concerns through ports (interfaces) and adapters (implementations). Proposed by Alistair Cockburn in 2005. The key insight is that the application should be equally drivable by users, programs, automated tests, or batch scripts, and equally developable and testable in isolation from its runtime devices and databases.
principles: Application core has no dependencies on frameworks or infrastructure
Ports define the interfaces the application exposes and requires
Adapters implement ports for specific technologies
Dependency rule: all dependencies point inward toward the core
The application is testable without any external infrastructure
when_to_use: Business logic is complex and must be protected from infrastructure churn
Multiple inbound channels (REST, CLI, events) drive the same business logic
Infrastructure components (databases, message brokers) are likely to change over time
Testability of business logic in isolation is a high priority
Team wants to defer infrastructure decisions without blocking domain development
avoid_when: Application is primarily CRUD with minimal business logic
The indirection overhead is not justified for simple data-pass-through services
Team is unfamiliar with dependency inversion and the added abstraction layers cause confusion
signals: Core domain code has zero imports from framework or infrastructure packages
Interface definitions (ports) exist at the boundary of the domain layer
Multiple adapter implementations exist for the same port (e.g., SQL adapter and in-memory adapter)
Tests run against the domain layer using in-memory adapters with no database or network
Changing the database requires modifying only the adapter, not the domain

patterns.name: Clean Architecture
patterns.description: A layered architecture where dependencies point inward from frameworks and drivers through interface adapters and use cases to the innermost entities layer. Proposed by Robert C. Martin in 2012 as a synthesis of hexagonal, onion, and DCI architectures. The key insight is the dependency rule: source code dependencies must only point inward, toward higher-level policies.
principles: Dependency rule: inner layers know nothing about outer layers
Entities encapsulate enterprise-wide business rules
Use cases encapsulate application-specific business rules
Interface adapters convert data between use case and external formats
Frameworks and drivers are details in the outermost ring
when_to_use: Long-lived enterprise application where business rules must survive technology changes
Multiple delivery mechanisms (web, mobile, CLI) share the same business logic
Team values testability and wants to test business rules without UI or database
Application has rich domain logic that justifies the layering overhead
avoid_when: Simple CRUD applications where the layering adds unnecessary complexity
Short-lived applications or prototypes where speed of delivery matters most
Team is small and the ceremony of multiple layers slows them down
signals: Concentric dependency layers where inner layers have no knowledge of outer layers
Entity classes contain only business rules with no framework annotations
Use case classes orchestrate entities and define application-specific workflows
Data crosses layer boundaries through DTOs or simple data structures, not entity objects
Framework code (controllers, repositories) lives in the outermost layer only

patterns.name: Onion Architecture
patterns.description: A layered architecture with the domain model at the center, surrounded by domain services, application services, and infrastructure at the outermost layer. Proposed by Jeffrey Palermo in 2008. The key insight is that infrastructure is a detail that wraps around the domain, inverting the traditional dependency direction where domain depended on data access.
principles: Domain model at the center with no outward dependencies
Layers wrap around the domain like an onion
Outer layers depend on inner layers, never the reverse
Infrastructure concerns live at the outermost ring
Interfaces defined in inner layers are implemented in outer layers
when_to_use: Domain-driven design is the primary modeling approach
Infrastructure persistence strategy is expected to change over the application lifetime
Team needs clear separation between domain logic and application orchestration
Rich domain model with complex invariants needs protection from infrastructure coupling
avoid_when: Application is data-centric with thin business logic
Team does not practice domain-driven design and the layers feel artificial
Rapid prototyping where layering slows iteration speed
signals: Domain model classes have no references to persistence, UI, or framework packages
Repository interfaces defined in the domain layer with implementations in infrastructure
Application services orchestrate domain objects and define transaction boundaries
Infrastructure layer contains ORM configurations, external API clients, and messaging adapters
Unit tests for domain logic require no infrastructure setup

patterns.name: Layered Architecture (N-Tier)
patterns.description: The classic architecture dividing an application into horizontal layers — typically presentation, business logic, and data access — where each layer may only communicate with the layer directly below it. One of the oldest and most widely used patterns. The key insight is separation of concerns through horizontal partitioning, though it often leads to changes requiring modifications across all layers.
principles: Each layer has a distinct responsibility
Layers communicate only with adjacent layers
Upper layers depend on lower layers
Each layer can be developed and tested somewhat independently
Layers can be physically distributed across tiers
when_to_use: Simple business applications with straightforward CRUD operations
Team is familiar with traditional three-tier development
Application requirements map naturally to presentation-logic-data separation
Quick start needed with well-understood patterns and abundant tooling support
avoid_when: Changes consistently require modifications across all layers (the 'layer tax')
Business logic is complex and does not fit neatly into a single horizontal layer
Cross-cutting concerns like caching or logging create layer-spanning complexity
Feature development is slowed by the need to touch every layer for each change
signals: Codebase is organized into presentation, service/business, and data access directories
Each layer has its own set of models or DTOs that map data between layers
Controllers call services, services call repositories — strict call hierarchy
Database access is isolated to the data access layer with no SQL in business logic
A new feature requires creating files in each layer (controller, service, repository)

patterns.name: Vertical Slice Architecture
patterns.description: An architecture organized around features rather than technical layers, where each feature (or slice) contains all the code needed from UI to database. Popularized by Jimmy Bogard. The key insight is that coupling within a feature is acceptable and natural, while coupling between features should be minimized — the opposite of layered architecture's priorities.
principles: Each feature is a self-contained vertical slice through all layers
Minimize coupling between slices, not between layers within a slice
Each slice can use whatever internal pattern is appropriate for its complexity
No shared abstractions forced across features
New features add new slices without modifying existing ones
when_to_use: Features have different complexity levels and forcing uniform layering wastes effort
Team wants to minimize the blast radius of changes to a single feature
CQRS or mediator patterns are already in use, providing natural slice boundaries
Product development is feature-driven with features rarely sharing business logic
avoid_when: Significant business logic is genuinely shared across many features
Team is unfamiliar with the pattern and defaults to inconsistent internal structures
Application is primarily CRUD where slices would all look identical
signals: Code is organized by feature directory rather than by technical layer
Each feature directory contains its own handler, validator, model, and persistence logic
Adding a new feature means adding a new directory, not modifying existing code
Different features use different internal patterns based on their complexity
Mediator or command/query dispatcher routes requests to feature handlers

patterns.name: Plugin Architecture
patterns.description: An architecture where a core application provides extension points that third-party or internal plugins can hook into to add functionality without modifying the core. Examples include Eclipse, VS Code, and webpack. The key insight is that the core defines the contracts and lifecycle, while plugins provide the variability — enabling an ecosystem of extensions without core instability.
principles: Core application defines stable extension points and plugin lifecycle
Plugins implement defined interfaces to extend functionality
Plugins are discovered and loaded at runtime
Core is stable and deployable without any plugins
Plugin isolation prevents one plugin from destabilizing another
when_to_use: Application needs to support third-party extensions without core modification
Different deployments need different feature sets composed from available plugins
Core product must remain stable while extension development moves independently
Building a platform or framework where extensibility is a primary value proposition
avoid_when: Extension points are not well defined and change frequently
Plugin isolation is hard to enforce and plugins can corrupt shared state
The overhead of plugin discovery, loading, and lifecycle management is not justified
signals: Formal plugin interface or abstract base class that all extensions must implement
Plugin discovery mechanism scans directories or registries at startup
Core application functions fully without any plugins installed
Plugin manifest files declare capabilities, dependencies, and version compatibility
Extension points are documented as a public API contract

patterns.name: Component-Based Architecture
patterns.description: An architecture where the system is decomposed into reusable, self-contained components with well-defined interfaces, encapsulating both behavior and data. Rooted in component-oriented programming from the 1990s (COM, JavaBeans, CORBA). The key insight is that components are units of composition — independently developed, versioned, and replaceable pieces that can be assembled into different configurations.
principles: Components are self-contained units with explicit interfaces
Components are independently replaceable and upgradeable
Component interactions happen only through defined interfaces
Components encapsulate their internal state and behavior
Components are reusable across different systems and contexts
when_to_use: System can be naturally decomposed into reusable units with clear interfaces
Multiple products or configurations need to be assembled from shared components
Different teams own different components and need clear interface contracts
Component marketplace or catalog is part of the business model
avoid_when: Component boundaries are unclear and interfaces change frequently
Overhead of formal interface definitions exceeds the benefit of reusability
Components are so tightly coupled that independent versioning is impractical
signals: System is assembled from independently versioned component packages
Each component exposes a public interface and hides its implementation
Components are published to and consumed from a component registry
Different system configurations use different combinations of the same components
Interface compatibility is checked at build time or deployment time

patterns.name: Event-Driven Architecture
patterns.description: An architecture where the flow of the program is determined by events — significant changes in state that are produced, detected, and consumed by loosely coupled components. Encompasses both event notification and event-carried state transfer patterns. The key insight is temporal decoupling: producers and consumers do not need to be active at the same time or know about each other.
principles: Components communicate by producing and consuming events
Producers do not know or care who consumes their events
Events are immutable facts representing something that happened
Temporal decoupling: producer and consumer lifecycles are independent
Event ordering and delivery guarantees are explicit architectural decisions
Events are the system's source of truth for what happened
when_to_use: Multiple independent consumers need to react to the same business event
System requires temporal decoupling — producers should not wait for consumers
Audit trail of all state changes is a business requirement
Real-time reactions to business events (notifications, analytics, cascading updates)
System spans multiple bounded contexts that should remain loosely coupled
avoid_when: Request-response semantics are needed and async event flows add unnecessary complexity
Debugging distributed event flows exceeds team capability
Event ordering is critical but the event infrastructure cannot guarantee it
System requires synchronous consistency and eventual consistency is unacceptable
signals: Message broker or event bus sits at the center of the architecture
Components publish events without knowledge of subscribers
Business processes are triggered by events rather than direct API calls
Event schema registry defines and versions event contracts
Dead letter queues handle failed event processing
Correlation IDs trace business transactions across event-driven flows

patterns.name: Command Query Responsibility Segregation (CQRS)
patterns.description: A pattern that separates read and write operations into different models, potentially backed by different data stores optimized for their respective access patterns. Introduced by Greg Young, building on Bertrand Meyer's CQS principle. The key insight is that read and write workloads have fundamentally different optimization needs and should not be forced into a single model.
principles: Commands (writes) and queries (reads) use separate models
Write model is optimized for consistency and business rule enforcement
Read model is optimized for query performance and denormalization
Read and write stores may be different technologies
Synchronization between models is eventually consistent via events
when_to_use: Read and write workloads have vastly different scaling requirements
Read model requires denormalized views that would be expensive to maintain in a normalized write model
Business logic on the write side is complex but reads are simple projections
Multiple read representations of the same data are needed for different consumers
Event sourcing is already in use and projections naturally separate reads from writes
avoid_when: Application is simple CRUD where read and write models would be nearly identical
Strong consistency between reads and writes is a hard requirement
Team is not prepared to handle eventual consistency and its edge cases
The additional complexity of maintaining two models is not justified by the workload difference
signals: Separate code paths for handling commands (mutations) and queries (reads)
Write model uses normalized schema while read model uses denormalized views
Events propagate changes from write store to read store asynchronously
Read endpoints return data from a different store than where writes are persisted
Query models are rebuilt or updated from event streams

patterns.name: Event Sourcing
patterns.description: A pattern where state changes are stored as an immutable sequence of events rather than storing current state directly. The current state is derived by replaying events from the beginning. Popularized by Greg Young and closely associated with CQRS. The key insight is that events are facts — they capture not just what the state is, but how it got there, enabling full auditability, temporal queries, and state reconstruction.
principles: State is stored as an ordered sequence of immutable events
Current state is derived by replaying events
Events represent business facts that have occurred
Event store is append-only — events are never modified or deleted
Snapshots optimize replay performance for long event streams
Events are the single source of truth
when_to_use: Complete audit trail of all state changes is a regulatory or business requirement
Need to reconstruct state at any point in time for debugging or compliance
Business domain naturally models processes as sequences of events
Multiple read models need to be built from the same event stream
Temporal queries (what was the state on date X) are a requirement
avoid_when: Simple CRUD where current state is all that matters
Event schema evolution is complex and team lacks experience with schema migration
Event replay for long-lived aggregates becomes prohibitively slow without snapshot management
Team is unfamiliar with eventual consistency and debugging event-sourced systems
signals: Append-only event store is the primary persistence mechanism
Aggregate state is reconstructed by replaying events from the store
Snapshot mechanism exists to optimize replay for long-lived aggregates
Event upcasters or schema evolution tooling handles event versioning
Projections build read-optimized views from the event stream
Business logic validates commands against current state derived from events

patterns.name: Saga Pattern (Orchestration)
patterns.description: A pattern for managing distributed transactions across multiple services using a central orchestrator that coordinates the sequence of local transactions and compensating actions. Based on the original saga concept by Garcia-Molina and Salem (1987). The key insight is that distributed transactions can be decomposed into a sequence of local transactions with compensating actions for rollback, coordinated by a single orchestrator.
principles: Central orchestrator defines and coordinates the saga flow
Each step is a local transaction within a single service
Compensating transactions undo completed steps on failure
Orchestrator maintains saga state and handles retries
Each participant service exposes both forward and compensating operations
when_to_use: Multi-service business process requires atomicity guarantees across services
Saga flow is complex with conditional branching and parallel steps
Central visibility into the state of long-running business processes is needed
New services are frequently added to the saga and the flow needs explicit management
Team prefers explicit flow definition over implicit choreography
avoid_when: Saga has only 2-3 simple sequential steps — choreography is simpler
Central orchestrator becomes a bottleneck or single point of failure
Services are owned by different organizations and a central orchestrator creates coupling
signals: Central saga orchestrator service defines step sequences and compensation logic
Saga state machine tracks the current step and status of each saga instance
Compensating transaction endpoints exist alongside forward transaction endpoints
Orchestrator persists saga state for crash recovery and observability
Saga execution logs show the ordered sequence of steps and their outcomes

patterns.name: Saga Pattern (Choreography)
patterns.description: A pattern for managing distributed transactions where each service publishes events that trigger the next step in the saga, with no central coordinator. Each service knows its own compensating action and listens for failure events. The key insight is that decentralized coordination through events avoids the single point of failure and coupling of a central orchestrator, at the cost of harder-to-trace saga flows.
principles: No central coordinator — each service reacts to events from other services
Each service publishes domain events after completing its local transaction
Services subscribe to relevant events and execute their part of the saga
Each service implements its own compensating logic triggered by failure events
The saga flow emerges from the event subscriptions rather than being explicitly defined
when_to_use: Saga flow is simple and linear with few branching points
Services are owned by different teams and no team wants to own a central orchestrator
Loose coupling between services is a higher priority than explicit flow visibility
Event infrastructure is already mature with reliable delivery guarantees
avoid_when: Saga has complex conditional logic, parallel branches, or many steps
End-to-end visibility into saga state is a hard requirement
Debugging implicit event-driven flows exceeds team capability
Adding new steps requires modifying event subscriptions across multiple services
signals: Services publish domain events that trigger actions in other services
No central saga coordinator exists — flow is implicit in event subscriptions
Each service has compensating event handlers for rollback scenarios
Distributed tracing with correlation IDs is used to follow saga execution
Event subscription configuration defines the implicit saga flow

patterns.name: Reactor Pattern
patterns.description: A concurrency pattern where a single-threaded event loop demultiplexes incoming I/O events and dispatches them to registered handlers. Used in Node.js, Nginx, and Redis. The key insight is that for I/O-bound workloads, a single-threaded event loop with non-blocking I/O can handle thousands of concurrent connections more efficiently than thread-per-connection models.
principles: Single-threaded event loop handles all I/O demultiplexing
Non-blocking I/O operations register callbacks for completion
Event handlers must be non-blocking to avoid starving other handlers
Synchronous demultiplexer (select/epoll/kqueue) waits for I/O readiness
CPU-intensive work must be offloaded to avoid blocking the event loop
when_to_use: High-concurrency I/O-bound workloads (web servers, proxies, chat servers)
Many concurrent connections with relatively little CPU work per request
Memory efficiency is critical — thread-per-connection uses too much memory
Workload consists of short-lived I/O operations with minimal computation
avoid_when: Workload is CPU-bound — single thread becomes the bottleneck
Long-running synchronous operations cannot be made non-blocking
Team is unfamiliar with callback-based or async programming models
signals: Central event loop dispatches I/O events to registered handlers
All I/O operations are non-blocking with callback or promise-based completion
Single thread handles thousands of concurrent connections
CPU-intensive tasks are explicitly offloaded to worker threads or processes
Event loop metrics show queue depth and handler execution time

patterns.name: Actor Model
patterns.description: A concurrency model where actors are the fundamental unit of computation. Each actor encapsulates state, processes messages sequentially from a mailbox, and can create other actors or send messages. Formalized by Carl Hewitt in 1973 and popularized by Erlang/OTP and Akka. The key insight is that shared mutable state is eliminated entirely — actors communicate only through asynchronous messages, making concurrency errors structurally impossible.
principles: Actors are the unit of computation with encapsulated state
Communication is exclusively through asynchronous message passing
Each actor processes one message at a time from its mailbox
Actors can create child actors, forming supervision hierarchies
No shared state between actors — all state is private
Supervision trees handle failure with let-it-crash philosophy
when_to_use: System requires massive concurrency with millions of lightweight concurrent entities
State isolation between concurrent entities is critical for correctness
Fault tolerance with automatic recovery from component failures is needed
System models real-world entities that maintain state and interact asynchronously
Geographic distribution where actors can transparently span network boundaries
avoid_when: Shared mutable state with strong consistency is a core requirement
Message passing overhead is unacceptable for the latency requirements
Team is unfamiliar with message-passing concurrency and debugging mailbox issues
signals: System entities are modeled as actors with private state and message handlers
Communication between entities is exclusively through asynchronous message passing
Actor supervision hierarchy defines failure recovery strategies
Mailbox queues buffer incoming messages for sequential processing
Actor system framework (Akka, Erlang/OTP, Microsoft Orleans) is in use
Location transparency allows actors to run on different nodes without code changes

patterns.name: Pipeline Pattern
patterns.description: A pattern where processing is decomposed into a sequence of stages, each performing a specific transformation, with the output of one stage feeding into the input of the next. Inspired by Unix pipes. The key insight is that complex processing can be decomposed into simple, composable, reusable stages that can be independently developed, tested, and potentially parallelized.
principles: Processing is a sequence of discrete stages
Each stage performs a single transformation
Output of one stage is input to the next
Stages are composable and reusable
Stages can potentially run in parallel on different data
when_to_use: Data processing naturally decomposes into sequential transformation steps
Stages need to be independently testable and reusable
Different pipelines share common stages in different configurations
Throughput can benefit from stage-level parallelism
avoid_when: Processing steps have complex interdependencies that break the linear flow
Feedback loops between stages are required
Pipeline overhead exceeds the benefit for very simple transformations
signals: Code is structured as a sequence of transformation functions chained together
Each stage has a uniform interface (input type, output type)
Pipeline configuration defines which stages run in which order
Stages are reused across different pipelines
Intermediate results between stages can be inspected for debugging

patterns.name: Chain of Responsibility
patterns.description: A behavioral pattern where a request is passed along a chain of handlers, each deciding whether to handle it or pass it to the next handler. Common in middleware stacks (Express.js, ASP.NET, Django). The key insight is that the sender of a request does not know which handler will process it, enabling dynamic composition of processing logic and open-ended extension.
principles: Request passes through a chain of handlers in order
Each handler decides to process, modify, or pass the request
Handlers are loosely coupled — each knows only the next handler
Chain can be composed and reordered at runtime
A handler can stop propagation or modify the request for downstream handlers
when_to_use: Multiple handlers may need to process the same request in sequence
The set of handlers needs to be configurable or extensible without modifying existing code
Cross-cutting concerns (auth, logging, validation) need to be composed flexibly
Request processing order matters and should be explicitly controllable
avoid_when: Every request is handled by the same handler — the chain adds unnecessary indirection
Chain becomes so long that debugging which handler processed a request is difficult
Performance-critical path where handler chain overhead is measurable
signals: Middleware stack where each middleware function calls next() to continue processing
Handler interface with a reference to the next handler in the chain
Request passes through multiple handlers before reaching the final handler
Handlers can be added, removed, or reordered through configuration
Each handler has a single responsibility (authentication, rate limiting, logging)

patterns.name: Mediator Pattern
patterns.description: A pattern where a central mediator object encapsulates how a set of objects interact, promoting loose coupling by preventing objects from referring to each other directly. In architecture, implemented as in-process message dispatchers like MediatR. The key insight is that many-to-many relationships between components are replaced by one-to-many relationships with the mediator, reducing coupling at the cost of centralizing interaction logic.
principles: Components communicate through the mediator, not directly
Mediator encapsulates interaction logic between components
Components know only the mediator interface, not each other
Adding new interaction patterns requires modifying the mediator, not the components
Reduces the number of direct dependencies between components
when_to_use: Multiple components have complex many-to-many interaction patterns
Need to centralize and manage interaction logic in one place
CQRS or vertical slice architecture uses in-process command/query dispatch
Components should be reusable independently of their interaction context
avoid_when: Mediator becomes a god object containing too much logic
Interactions are simple point-to-point and the mediator adds unnecessary indirection
Performance-critical path where mediator dispatch overhead matters
signals: Central dispatcher routes commands and queries to appropriate handlers
Components send messages to the mediator rather than calling each other directly
Handler registration maps message types to handler implementations
Adding new behavior means adding new message types and handlers, not modifying existing components
Pipeline behaviors in the mediator handle cross-cutting concerns

patterns.name: Observer Pattern
patterns.description: A pattern where an object (subject) maintains a list of dependents (observers) and notifies them of state changes, typically by calling one of their methods. One of the original GoF patterns and the foundation for reactive programming. The key insight is that the subject does not need to know the concrete types of its observers, enabling open-ended extension of reaction logic.
principles: Subject maintains a list of observers and notifies them of changes
Observers register and unregister dynamically
Subject knows observers only through a common interface
Notification is typically synchronous within a single process
Push vs pull models define how much data observers receive
when_to_use: State changes in one object need to trigger updates in multiple dependent objects
The set of dependent objects changes dynamically at runtime
Decoupling the subject from the concrete types of its dependents is important
Reactive UI frameworks where view components observe model changes
avoid_when: Observer list grows unbounded causing notification performance issues
Cascading notifications create update storms or infinite loops
Memory leaks from forgotten observer registrations are a recurring problem
signals: Subject class has subscribe/unsubscribe methods for observers
Observer interface defines an update or notify method
State changes trigger iteration over observer list with notifications
Observers can be added or removed at runtime
Event emitter pattern with named events and listener registration

patterns.name: State Machine
patterns.description: A pattern where an entity's behavior is governed by its current state, with explicitly defined transitions between states triggered by events. Used extensively in protocol implementations, workflow engines, and UI state management. The key insight is that making states and transitions explicit eliminates impossible state bugs and makes the system's behavior verifiable and visualizable.
principles: All valid states are explicitly enumerated
Transitions between states are triggered by events and are explicit
Actions are associated with transitions, not states alone
Invalid transitions are structurally prevented
Current state determines which events are accepted
when_to_use: Entity has a well-defined lifecycle with distinct states and transitions
Need to enforce that only valid state transitions occur
Business process has complex conditional logic based on current state
Visualization of state flow is valuable for communication with stakeholders
Protocol implementation with defined state transitions
avoid_when: State space is too large to enumerate explicitly (use rules engine instead)
Transitions are so simple that a state machine adds unnecessary formalism
Entity behavior does not vary based on state
signals: Enum or constant set defines all valid states for an entity
Transition table or transition functions map (state, event) to next state
Guard conditions on transitions enforce business rules
State machine visualization (state diagram) documents the entity lifecycle
Invalid state transitions throw explicit errors rather than silently failing

patterns.name: Command Pattern
patterns.description: A pattern that encapsulates a request as an object, allowing parameterization of clients with different requests, queuing, logging, and undo/redo support. One of the original GoF patterns, widely used in CQRS and task-based architectures. The key insight is that reifying operations as objects enables them to be stored, transmitted, queued, and undone — capabilities impossible with direct method calls.
principles: Each operation is encapsulated as a command object
Commands are self-contained with all data needed for execution
Commands can be serialized, queued, logged, and replayed
Undo is supported by storing inverse operations or previous state
Command handler is separate from the command definition
when_to_use: Operations need to be queued, scheduled, or executed asynchronously
Undo/redo functionality is a requirement
Operations need to be logged for audit or replay purposes
Task-based UI where user actions map to domain commands
CQRS write side where mutations are modeled as explicit commands
avoid_when: Operations are simple synchronous calls with no need for queuing or undo
Command object overhead is unjustified for trivial operations
Command proliferation creates too many small classes with little logic
signals: Operations are represented as objects with execute() methods
Command queue or command bus dispatches commands to handlers
Undo stack stores executed commands for reversal
Command objects carry all parameters needed for execution
Command handlers are registered separately from command definitions

patterns.name: Serverless Architecture
patterns.description: An architecture where the cloud provider manages server infrastructure, scaling, and availability, while developers deploy individual functions or containers that run on demand. Pioneered by AWS Lambda in 2014. The key insight is that operational burden shifts entirely to the provider, enabling teams to focus on business logic at the cost of vendor coupling and cold start latency.
principles: No server management — provider handles infrastructure
Pay-per-invocation pricing model
Automatic scaling from zero to peak demand
Stateless function execution — state lives in external stores
Event-triggered invocation from various event sources
Short-lived execution with provider-defined time limits
when_to_use: Workload is spiky with long idle periods — pay-per-use is cost-effective
Team wants to eliminate server management and operational overhead
Event-driven workloads with well-defined triggers (HTTP, queue, schedule)
Rapid prototyping where infrastructure setup would slow development
Glue logic connecting managed services (S3 to DynamoDB, API Gateway to Lambda)
avoid_when: Workload is steady-state with high utilization — serverless is more expensive than reserved instances
Cold start latency is unacceptable for the use case
Functions need long execution times exceeding provider limits
Vendor lock-in is unacceptable and portability is a requirement
Complex stateful workflows are required within a single execution
signals: Functions deployed to AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, or Google Cloud Functions
Infrastructure-as-code defines event source mappings and function configurations
No long-running server processes — all compute is on-demand
State is stored in managed services (DynamoDB, S3, Cosmos DB) not local disk
Cold start metrics appear in production monitoring dashboards
Function concurrency limits and throttling configuration exists

patterns.name: Containerized Architecture
patterns.description: An architecture where applications are packaged as container images and deployed to container orchestration platforms like Kubernetes. Docker (2013) and Kubernetes (2014) established the pattern. The key insight is that containers provide a consistent deployment unit across environments, decoupling the application from the host OS while being lighter than virtual machines.
principles: Applications are packaged as immutable container images
Container orchestrator manages scheduling, scaling, and health
Infrastructure is defined as code (Kubernetes manifests, Helm charts)
Containers are ephemeral and can be replaced at any time
Service discovery and load balancing are provided by the platform
Resource limits and isolation are enforced per container
when_to_use: Need consistent deployment across development, staging, and production environments
Multiple services with different runtime requirements share the same infrastructure
Auto-scaling based on resource utilization or custom metrics is needed
Rolling deployments with zero downtime are a requirement
Team needs fine-grained resource allocation and isolation
avoid_when: Single simple application where container orchestration overhead is not justified
Team lacks Kubernetes or container orchestration expertise
Workload requires direct hardware access that containers cannot provide
Operational overhead of managing a container platform exceeds the benefit
signals: Dockerfiles define application packaging and dependencies
Kubernetes manifests or Helm charts define deployment configurations
Container registry stores versioned container images
Health checks and readiness probes ensure container availability
Horizontal pod autoscaler adjusts replica count based on metrics
Service mesh or ingress controller manages traffic routing

patterns.name: Cell-Based Architecture
patterns.description: An architecture where the system is deployed as multiple independent cells, each containing a complete copy of the application stack and serving a subset of traffic (typically by tenant, region, or shard). Used by AWS internally and adopted by large SaaS providers. The key insight is that blast radius containment is achieved by isolating failure domains — a cell failure affects only the traffic routed to that cell.
principles: Each cell is a complete, independent deployment of the application stack
Cells are isolated — failure in one cell does not affect others
Traffic is routed to cells by a thin routing layer (by tenant, region, or hash)
Cells are identical in code but independent in state and infrastructure
Cell size is bounded to limit blast radius
New cells are deployed for capacity expansion, not by scaling existing cells
when_to_use: Blast radius containment is critical — an outage must not affect all customers
Multi-tenant SaaS where tenant isolation is a security or compliance requirement
System serves millions of users and total failure would be catastrophic
Need to deploy updates to a subset of cells first as a deployment safety mechanism
avoid_when: System is small enough that single-deployment redundancy is sufficient
Cross-cell queries or aggregations are frequently needed
Operational cost of maintaining multiple identical deployments is prohibitive
Team lacks automation to manage cell provisioning and routing
signals: Multiple identical deployments serve different subsets of traffic
Routing layer directs requests to specific cells based on tenant or region
Cell health monitoring triggers traffic rerouting on cell failure
Each cell has its own database, cache, and compute resources
Deployment pipeline can target individual cells or roll out across cells sequentially

patterns.name: Multi-Region Deployment
patterns.description: An architecture where the application is deployed across multiple geographic regions, providing low latency to global users and resilience to regional outages. The key insight is that geography introduces fundamental constraints — speed of light latency, data sovereignty laws, and regional failure independence — that must be addressed at the architecture level, not just the infrastructure level.
principles: Application is deployed in multiple geographic regions
Data replication strategy handles cross-region consistency
Traffic routing directs users to the nearest healthy region
Each region can operate independently during a regional outage
Data sovereignty and compliance requirements are respected per region
when_to_use: Global user base requires low latency from multiple continents
Business continuity requires surviving a full regional cloud outage
Data sovereignty regulations require data to remain in specific regions
Compliance standards mandate geographic redundancy
avoid_when: User base is concentrated in a single region
Strong consistency across regions is a hard requirement — cross-region latency makes this expensive
Operational complexity of multi-region deployment exceeds team capability
Cost of running redundant infrastructure in multiple regions is prohibitive
signals: Infrastructure-as-code provisions identical stacks in multiple cloud regions
Global load balancer routes traffic based on geographic proximity
Database replication runs across regions with defined consistency guarantees
Failover playbooks define how to redirect traffic during regional outages
Regional health checks trigger automatic failover to healthy regions

patterns.name: Blue-Green Deployment
patterns.description: A deployment strategy maintaining two identical production environments (blue and green), where one serves live traffic while the other is updated and tested. Traffic is switched atomically from one to the other. The key insight is that deployment risk is minimized by preparing the new version in a full production-equivalent environment and switching traffic only after validation, with instant rollback by switching back.
principles: Two identical production environments exist simultaneously
One environment serves live traffic while the other is staged
Traffic switch is atomic — all users move at once
Rollback is instant by switching traffic back to the previous environment
Database schema changes must be backward-compatible across both versions
when_to_use: Zero-downtime deployments are required
Need instant rollback capability without redeployment
Full production validation of the new version before exposing to users
Deployment window needs to be as short as possible
avoid_when: Cost of maintaining two full production environments is prohibitive
Database schema changes are not backward-compatible between versions
Stateful components make environment switching complex
Gradual traffic shifting is preferred over all-at-once switching
signals: Two production environments labeled blue and green in infrastructure configuration
Load balancer or DNS configuration determines which environment receives traffic
Deployment pipeline deploys to the inactive environment and runs validation tests
Traffic cutover is a single configuration change (DNS, load balancer weight)
Both environments share the same database or use compatible schema versions

patterns.name: Canary Deployment
patterns.description: A deployment strategy where a new version is gradually rolled out to a small percentage of users before full deployment. Traffic percentage increases as confidence grows based on metrics and error rates. Named after canaries in coal mines. The key insight is that exposing a small percentage of real traffic catches production issues that testing environments cannot surface, while limiting blast radius.
principles: New version receives a small percentage of production traffic initially
Traffic percentage increases gradually based on health metrics
Automated rollback triggers on error rate or latency threshold breaches
Old and new versions run simultaneously during the rollout
Canary metrics are compared against baseline for anomaly detection
when_to_use: Changes have production risk that cannot be fully validated in staging
Gradual rollout with automated rollback is preferred over all-at-once deployment
Metrics infrastructure can detect anomalies in real-time during rollout
User impact of bugs must be minimized to a small percentage
avoid_when: System lacks metrics infrastructure for real-time comparison between versions
Both versions must be schema-compatible and the schema change is breaking
Traffic volume is too low for statistically meaningful canary comparison
signals: Traffic splitting configuration routes a percentage of requests to the new version
Canary analysis job compares error rates and latency between old and new versions
Automated rollback triggers when canary metrics exceed thresholds
Deployment pipeline has graduated traffic increase stages (1%, 5%, 25%, 100%)
Canary deployment status dashboard shows real-time comparison metrics

patterns.name: Feature Flags (Feature Toggles)
patterns.description: A technique where features are wrapped in conditional flags that can be toggled at runtime without deployment, enabling trunk-based development and controlled feature rollout. Systematized by Martin Fowler's feature toggle taxonomy. The key insight is that deployment and release are decoupled — code can be deployed without being activated, and activation can target specific users, percentages, or segments.
principles: Features are wrapped in runtime-evaluable toggle conditions
Toggles decouple deployment from feature release
Toggle state is managed externally (config service, database, feature flag platform)
Toggles can target specific users, segments, or percentages
Toggle lifecycle management ensures stale flags are cleaned up
when_to_use: Trunk-based development where incomplete features must be in main without being active
Gradual feature rollout to specific user segments (beta users, regions, plans)
A/B testing requires serving different experiences to different user groups
Kill switch capability to disable features instantly without deployment
Long-running feature branches need to be eliminated
avoid_when: Toggle management overhead exceeds the benefit — tech debt from stale toggles accumulates
Testing matrix explodes with too many toggle combinations
Team lacks discipline to remove toggles after features are fully released
signals: Feature flag evaluation calls in application code gate feature access
Feature flag management platform (LaunchDarkly, Unleash, Flagsmith) is in use
Toggle configuration includes targeting rules for user segments
Toggle cleanup tracking ensures stale flags are identified and removed
Testing framework supports running tests with different toggle combinations

patterns.name: Sidecar Pattern
patterns.description: A pattern where auxiliary functionality is deployed as a separate process (sidecar) alongside the main application container, sharing the same lifecycle and network namespace. Popularized by service mesh implementations like Envoy/Istio. The key insight is that cross-cutting concerns (logging, monitoring, mTLS, retries) can be extracted from application code into a co-located sidecar, enabling polyglot applications to share infrastructure capabilities.
principles: Sidecar runs alongside the main container sharing the same pod/host
Sidecar handles cross-cutting concerns transparently
Main application is unaware of sidecar functionality
Sidecar lifecycle is coupled to the main application lifecycle
Communication between main app and sidecar is via localhost
when_to_use: Cross-cutting concerns need to be consistent across polyglot services
Service mesh capabilities (mTLS, retries, circuit breaking) need to be added without code changes
Logging, metrics, and tracing collection should be uniform across all services
Main application should not be responsible for infrastructure concerns
avoid_when: Resource overhead of running an additional container per service is unacceptable
Sidecar adds latency to every request on the critical path
Application is a monolith where sidecar concerns are handled in-process efficiently
signals: Pod specification includes multiple containers: main application and sidecar
Envoy, Istio, or Linkerd sidecar proxy is injected into every service pod
Network traffic is intercepted by the sidecar proxy transparently
Sidecar containers appear in pod resource allocations alongside main containers
Service mesh control plane configures sidecar behavior centrally

patterns.name: Ambassador Pattern
patterns.description: A pattern where a helper service acts as an out-of-process proxy for a client, handling cross-cutting communication concerns like retries, circuit breaking, and routing. Similar to sidecar but focused on outbound communication. The key insight is that legacy applications or applications in languages without good client libraries can gain advanced communication capabilities through a co-located proxy.
principles: Ambassador proxy sits between the application and remote services
Handles outbound communication concerns (retries, timeouts, circuit breaking)
Application calls the ambassador on localhost instead of remote services
Ambassador can be updated independently of the application
Provides advanced features without modifying application code
when_to_use: Legacy application needs modern communication resilience without code changes
Application language lacks good client libraries for circuit breaking or retries
Outbound traffic needs monitoring, routing, or transformation uniformly
Need to add TLS termination or authentication to outbound calls transparently
avoid_when: Application has good native client libraries with built-in resilience
Latency overhead of the proxy is unacceptable
Single language environment where a shared library is simpler
signals: Outbound service calls route through a localhost proxy rather than directly to remote services
Proxy configuration defines retry policies, circuit breaker thresholds, and timeouts
Application code uses simple HTTP calls to localhost while proxy handles complex routing
Ambassador container runs alongside the application in the same pod
Proxy metrics show outbound request latency, retry counts, and circuit breaker state

patterns.name: Strangler Fig Pattern
patterns.description: A migration pattern where a legacy system is incrementally replaced by routing specific functionality to a new system while the old system continues to handle everything else. Named by Martin Fowler after strangler fig trees that grow around and eventually replace their host tree. The key insight is that big-bang rewrites almost always fail — incremental migration with parallel running reduces risk and delivers value continuously.
principles: New system is built incrementally alongside the legacy system
Routing layer directs specific requests to old or new system
Functionality migrates one capability at a time
Old system is decommissioned only after all functionality has migrated
Rollback is possible by rerouting traffic back to the legacy system
when_to_use: Replacing a legacy system that cannot be rewritten all at once
Need to deliver value incrementally during a multi-year migration
Risk of big-bang rewrite failure is unacceptable
Legacy system must remain operational throughout the migration
Migrating from monolith to microservices incrementally
avoid_when: Legacy system is small enough for a complete rewrite in a single sprint
Routing layer between old and new systems is impractical
Data synchronization between old and new systems during migration is too complex
signals: Reverse proxy or API gateway routes some URLs to the legacy system and others to the new system
Migration progress tracker shows which capabilities have been migrated
Feature parity tests verify new implementation matches legacy behavior
Data synchronization mechanism keeps old and new systems consistent during migration
Traffic gradually shifts from legacy to new system as capabilities are migrated

patterns.name: Branch by Abstraction
patterns.description: A technique for making large-scale changes to a system in a gradual way that allows you to release the system regularly while the change is in progress. An abstraction layer is introduced, with the old implementation behind it initially, then a new implementation is built alongside, and finally traffic is switched. Described by Paul Hammant. The key insight is that long-lived feature branches are avoided by using abstraction layers as the branching mechanism within the codebase itself.
principles: Introduce an abstraction layer over the component to be replaced
Old implementation initially serves all traffic through the abstraction
New implementation is built behind the same abstraction
Traffic is gradually switched from old to new implementation
Old implementation is removed after full migration
when_to_use: Need to replace a core component without long-lived feature branches
Trunk-based development requires all changes to be on main
Replacement work spans multiple sprints and must not block other development
Need to A/B test old vs new implementation during migration
avoid_when: Abstraction layer is difficult to introduce because the component is deeply entangled
Performance overhead of the abstraction layer is unacceptable
Replacement can be done quickly enough that branching is simpler
signals: Interface or abstraction layer wraps the component being replaced
Both old and new implementations exist behind the same interface
Configuration or feature flag determines which implementation is active
Tests run against both implementations to verify behavioral parity
Old implementation is removed after migration is confirmed complete

patterns.name: Pipe and Filter
patterns.description: An architecture where data flows through a sequence of processing components (filters) connected by channels (pipes). Each filter transforms the data independently without knowledge of other filters. Inspired by Unix pipe philosophy. The key insight is that filters are composable, reusable, and independently deployable processing stages connected by standardized data channels.
principles: Filters are independent processing components with uniform interfaces
Pipes connect filter outputs to filter inputs
Filters are stateless or manage only local state
Filters can be added, removed, or reordered without modifying other filters
Each filter performs a single transformation
when_to_use: Data processing consists of independent transformation stages
Need to reuse processing stages across different data flows
Parallel processing of stages on streaming data is desirable
System processes data in a natural linear or fan-out flow
avoid_when: Processing requires feedback loops between stages
Shared state between filters is needed for the transformation logic
Overhead of data serialization between pipes is prohibitive
signals: Processing components implement a common transform interface (input -> output)
Components are connected via queues, streams, or channels
Processing flow is defined by configuration, not hardcoded connections
Individual components can be tested with mock input and verified output
Fan-out or fan-in topologies split and merge data flows

patterns.name: Batch Sequential Processing
patterns.description: An architecture where data is processed in discrete batches, with each processing step completing entirely before the next begins. The output of each step is typically written to persistent storage before the next step reads it. One of the oldest data processing patterns. The key insight is that batch boundaries provide natural checkpoints for restart and recovery, and batch processing enables efficient bulk operations that streaming cannot match.
principles: Data is processed in discrete batches, not individual records
Each step completes entirely before the next begins
Intermediate results are persisted between steps for recovery
Batch scheduling controls when processing runs
Batch size and frequency are tunable parameters
when_to_use: Data arrives in natural batches (daily files, hourly dumps, periodic exports)
Processing requires complete dataset access (sorting, aggregation, global statistics)
Real-time processing is not required and batch latency is acceptable
Bulk database operations are more efficient than row-by-row processing
avoid_when: Real-time or near-real-time processing is required
Data volume per batch is growing faster than processing capacity
Users expect immediate reflection of changes in query results
signals: Scheduled jobs (cron, Airflow, SSIS) trigger processing at defined intervals
Intermediate data is written to staging tables or files between steps
Batch job monitoring tracks completion status and processing duration
Retry logic restarts from the last successful step, not from the beginning
Processing window constraints define when batch jobs may run

patterns.name: Stream Processing Architecture
patterns.description: An architecture where data is processed continuously as it arrives, event by event or in micro-batches, rather than in large periodic batches. Exemplified by Apache Kafka Streams, Apache Flink, and Apache Spark Streaming. The key insight is that treating data as a continuous stream enables real-time insights and reactions, with the event log serving as the source of truth.
principles: Data is processed as a continuous stream of events
Processing happens in near-real-time as events arrive
Event ordering and time semantics (event time vs processing time) are explicit
Exactly-once or at-least-once processing guarantees are defined
Windowing operations aggregate events over time intervals
Stream processing topology defines the dataflow graph
when_to_use: Real-time analytics, dashboards, or alerting on live data
Event-driven reactions must happen within seconds or milliseconds
Data volume makes batch processing windows too long
Continuous ETL from operational systems to analytics systems
Complex event processing (pattern detection across event streams)
avoid_when: Processing requires access to the complete dataset (full sorts, global aggregations)
Data arrives in natural batches and real-time processing adds no value
Operational complexity of streaming infrastructure exceeds team capability
signals: Apache Kafka, Kinesis, or Pulsar serves as the event streaming platform
Stream processing jobs run continuously (Flink, Kafka Streams, Spark Streaming)
Windowing operations (tumbling, sliding, session) aggregate events over time
Consumer group management handles partition assignment and rebalancing
Backpressure mechanisms prevent fast producers from overwhelming slow consumers
Stream processing lag metrics track how far behind real-time the processing is

patterns.name: ETL Pipeline
patterns.description: A data integration pattern that extracts data from source systems, transforms it to fit operational or analytical needs, and loads it into a destination data store. The foundational pattern of data warehousing since the 1980s. The key insight is that source systems and analytical systems have fundamentally different schemas and quality requirements, and transformation bridges this gap.
principles: Extract: read data from source systems without modifying them
Transform: clean, validate, denormalize, and reshape data
Load: write transformed data to the destination store
Idempotent operations enable safe re-execution
Data lineage tracking shows where each piece of data came from
when_to_use: Data needs to move from operational databases to analytical data warehouses
Source data requires cleaning, validation, and schema transformation
Multiple source systems need to be integrated into a unified data model
Regulatory requirements mandate data quality checks before loading
avoid_when: Real-time data availability is required — ETL batch latency is too high
Transformation logic is simple enough that ELT (load then transform) is better
Source systems can expose data through APIs, making extraction unnecessary
signals: ETL tool or framework (Airflow, dbt, SSIS, Informatica) orchestrates data pipelines
Source connectors extract data from databases, APIs, or files
Transformation logic cleans, validates, and reshapes data between extract and load
Data quality checks validate records before loading to the destination
Pipeline monitoring tracks extraction lag, transformation errors, and load throughput

patterns.name: Lambda Architecture
patterns.description: A data processing architecture with three layers: a batch layer for comprehensive but high-latency processing, a speed layer for low-latency approximate processing, and a serving layer that merges results from both. Proposed by Nathan Marz. The key insight is that batch processing provides accuracy while stream processing provides speed, and combining them provides both — at the cost of maintaining two separate processing codebases.
principles: Batch layer processes the complete dataset for accuracy
Speed layer processes recent data for low latency
Serving layer merges batch and speed layer results
Immutable master dataset is the source of truth
Speed layer results are eventually replaced by batch layer results
when_to_use: Need both real-time approximate results and accurate batch-computed results
Complete reprocessing of historical data is needed when logic changes
Use case tolerates temporary inconsistency between real-time and batch views
Existing batch infrastructure needs to be augmented with real-time capabilities
avoid_when: Maintaining two codebases (batch and stream) for the same logic is too costly
Kappa architecture can meet latency requirements with stream processing alone
Team lacks expertise in both batch and stream processing technologies
signals: Parallel batch and stream processing pipelines process the same data
Batch layer runs periodically over the complete dataset (Hadoop, Spark batch)
Speed layer processes events in real-time (Storm, Flink, Spark Streaming)
Serving layer merges batch views with speed layer results for queries
Two implementations of the same transformation logic exist (batch and stream)

patterns.name: Kappa Architecture
patterns.description: A simplification of lambda architecture that uses a single stream processing layer for both real-time and historical data processing. Historical reprocessing is achieved by replaying the event log through updated stream processors. Proposed by Jay Kreps. The key insight is that if the event log is retained, stream processing alone can serve both real-time and batch needs, eliminating the dual-codebase problem of lambda architecture.
principles: Single stream processing layer handles all data processing
Immutable event log retains all historical events
Reprocessing replays events through updated stream processors
No separate batch layer — stream processing handles everything
Event log retention period determines reprocessing capability
when_to_use: Stream processing technology can handle both real-time and reprocessing workloads
Maintaining dual codebases (batch + stream) is unacceptable
Event log can be retained long enough for reprocessing needs
Processing logic is the same for real-time and historical data
avoid_when: Historical reprocessing requires complete dataset access that event replay cannot provide
Event log retention costs are prohibitive for the data volume
Processing logic fundamentally differs between real-time and batch (different algorithms)
signals: Single stream processing pipeline handles both real-time and historical data
Event log (Kafka) retains events for extended periods enabling replay
Reprocessing is triggered by deploying updated stream processors and replaying events
No separate batch processing infrastructure exists
Stream processor state stores serve queries directly

patterns.name: Data Mesh
patterns.description: A decentralized data architecture where domain teams own and serve their data as products, with a federated governance model and self-serve data platform. Proposed by Zhamak Dehghani in 2019. The key insight is that centralizing data ownership in a single data team creates a bottleneck — instead, data ownership should follow domain ownership, with domains publishing data products for consumption.
principles: Domain-oriented data ownership — each domain owns its data products
Data as a product — domains treat data consumers as customers
Self-serve data platform provides tooling for domain teams
Federated computational governance ensures interoperability
Data product contracts define SLAs, schemas, and quality guarantees
when_to_use: Centralized data team is a bottleneck for data delivery across the organization
Organization has well-defined domain teams that understand their data best
Multiple domains need to share data but a central data warehouse creates coupling
Data quality is best enforced by the teams that produce the data
avoid_when: Organization is small and a single data team can handle all data needs
Domain teams lack the skills or willingness to own data products
Federated governance overhead exceeds the benefit of decentralization
Data volume and complexity do not justify the architectural investment
signals: Domain teams publish data products with documented schemas and SLAs
Self-serve data platform provides tools for data product creation and discovery
Data product catalog enables discovery across domain boundaries
Federated governance policies are enforced computationally, not manually
Domain data products have their own CI/CD pipelines and quality checks

patterns.name: Change Data Capture (CDC)
patterns.description: A pattern that captures row-level changes (inserts, updates, deletes) from a database and publishes them as events for downstream consumers. Implemented by tools like Debezium reading database transaction logs. The key insight is that the database transaction log already contains a complete, ordered record of all changes — reading it avoids polling, triggers, and application-level change tracking.
principles: Changes are captured from the database transaction log, not from application code
Captures inserts, updates, and deletes as discrete events
Preserves the order of changes as they occurred in the source database
Does not modify or impact the source database performance significantly
Provides both initial snapshot and ongoing change stream
when_to_use: Need to replicate data from operational databases to analytics or search systems
Event-driven architecture needs events from legacy systems that do not emit them
Database synchronization between systems without application-level change tracking
Building read replicas or materialized views from operational data
avoid_when: Source database does not expose a transaction log or the log format is proprietary
Application-level events already capture all changes and CDC adds redundancy
Change volume is so high that the CDC pipeline cannot keep up
signals: Debezium, Maxwell, or similar CDC connector reads database transaction logs
Change events are published to Kafka or similar event streaming platform
CDC connector configuration specifies which tables and schemas to capture
Initial snapshot followed by continuous change streaming is the setup pattern
Downstream consumers process change events to update derived data stores

patterns.name: Materialized View Pattern
patterns.description: A pattern where pre-computed query results are stored as a concrete data structure, updated when underlying data changes. Used in databases as materialized views and in application architecture as read-optimized projections. The key insight is that trading storage for compute eliminates expensive joins and aggregations at query time, shifting the cost to write time or background refresh.
principles: Query results are pre-computed and stored, not computed on each request
Views are updated when underlying data changes (synchronous or async)
Multiple materialized views can serve different query patterns from the same source
Staleness is an explicit trade-off — refresh frequency balances freshness and cost
Views are rebuilt from source data, not patched
when_to_use: Expensive queries with complex joins or aggregations are executed frequently
Read-heavy workloads where pre-computation amortizes the cost across many reads
CQRS read models that serve denormalized views of the write model
Dashboard and reporting queries that do not need real-time data
avoid_when: Underlying data changes so frequently that view maintenance dominates write performance
Query patterns are ad-hoc and unpredictable — cannot pre-compute all possible views
Storage cost of materialized views exceeds compute cost savings
signals: Database materialized views or application-level projection tables store pre-computed results
Background jobs or event handlers refresh materialized views on data changes
Read endpoints query materialized views rather than computing joins on the fly
View refresh lag metrics track how stale the materialized data is
Multiple denormalized views serve different query patterns from the same source data

patterns.name: Write-Ahead Log (WAL)
patterns.description: A pattern where all modifications are written to a sequential, append-only log before being applied to the main data structure.
