Used in databases (PostgreSQL WAL, MySQL redo log) and distributed systems (Raft log, Kafka). The key insight is that sequential writes to a log are orders of magnitude faster than random writes to data structures, and the log provides durability and crash recovery guarantees.
principles: All modifications are appended to the log before being applied
Log entries are sequential and append-only
Recovery replays the log from the last checkpoint
Log is the source of truth until changes are flushed to main storage
Checkpointing periodically saves state to reduce recovery time
when_to_use: Crash recovery must guarantee no data loss for committed operations
Sequential write performance is needed for write-heavy workloads
Replication requires an ordered log of changes to propagate to replicas
Transactional guarantees (atomicity, durability) must be maintained across crashes
avoid_when: Write volume is low and direct writes to the data structure are fast enough
Storage overhead of maintaining the log is prohibitive
System does not need crash recovery guarantees
signals: Append-only log file exists alongside the main data files
Write operations append to the log before modifying the main data structure
Crash recovery procedure replays log entries from the last checkpoint
Log compaction or checkpointing periodically truncates old log entries
Replication reads from the log to propagate changes to follower nodes

patterns.name: Monolith to Microservices Migration
patterns.description: A migration strategy pattern for incrementally decomposing a monolithic application into microservices. Described extensively by Sam Newman. The key insight is that successful migration requires identifying seam points in the existing codebase, extracting along domain boundaries, and maintaining the monolith as a functioning system throughout the transition — not attempting a parallel rewrite.
principles: Identify seam points along domain boundaries in the existing monolith
Extract one bounded context at a time into a separate service
Maintain the monolith as a functioning system throughout migration
Use strangler fig pattern to incrementally route traffic to new services
Database decomposition follows service decomposition
when_to_use: Monolith has become too large for a single team to maintain and deploy
Different parts of the monolith need independent scaling or deployment
Organization is growing and teams need autonomous service ownership
Technical debt in the monolith prevents rapid feature delivery
avoid_when: Monolith is still manageable and the migration cost exceeds the benefit
Domain boundaries are not well understood — premature extraction creates a distributed monolith
Team lacks operational readiness for distributed systems
signals: Monolith codebase is being analyzed for bounded context boundaries
New services are being extracted one at a time from the monolith
API gateway routes some requests to new services and others to the monolith
Database tables are being migrated from the shared monolith database to service-owned databases
Anti-corruption layer mediates between monolith and new service data models

patterns.name: Backends for Frontends (BFF)
patterns.description: A pattern where each frontend application (web, mobile, IoT) has its own dedicated backend service that aggregates and shapes data specifically for that frontend's needs. Described by Sam Newman and Phil Calçado. The key insight is that different frontends have fundamentally different data needs — a mobile app needs compact responses while a web dashboard needs rich aggregations — and a single general-purpose API cannot serve all optimally.
principles: Each frontend type has its own dedicated backend service
BFF aggregates calls to downstream services for its frontend
BFF shapes response data to match its frontend's specific needs
Frontend team owns their BFF, not a separate backend team
BFF contains only presentation logic, not business logic
when_to_use: Different frontends (mobile, web, third-party) need significantly different API shapes
General-purpose API is becoming bloated with frontend-specific logic
Frontend teams want to control their own backend without coordinating with a central API team
Mobile and web have different performance constraints requiring different response shapes
avoid_when: Single frontend type — a BFF adds an unnecessary layer
Frontend API needs are similar enough that a single API serves all well
BFF starts containing business logic that should live in downstream services
signals: Separate backend services exist per frontend type (web-bff, mobile-bff, iot-bff)
BFF aggregates multiple downstream service calls into a single frontend-optimized response
Frontend team repository includes the BFF code alongside frontend code
BFF responses are tailored to frontend needs (field selection, nesting, pagination)
API gateway routes to different BFF services based on client type headers

patterns.name: Micro Frontends
patterns.description: An architecture that extends microservice principles to the frontend, where the web application is composed of features independently developed, tested, and deployed by different teams. Each micro frontend owns a vertical slice of the UI. The key insight is that frontend monoliths create the same bottlenecks as backend monoliths — independent team autonomy requires independent frontend deployment.
principles: Each micro frontend is independently developed, tested, and deployed
Team owns a vertical slice from UI to backend
Micro frontends are composed at build time, server time, or runtime
Shared nothing architecture — each micro frontend manages its own state
Shell application provides routing and composition infrastructure
when_to_use: Multiple teams work on different features of the same web application
Teams need to deploy frontend changes independently without coordinating releases
Different features benefit from different frontend frameworks or versions
Product is assembled from independently developed feature modules
avoid_when: Single team owns the entire frontend — micro frontend overhead is unjustified
Consistent UX requires tight coupling between features that micro frontends make harder
Performance overhead of loading multiple frameworks degrades user experience
signals: Web application is composed from independently deployed frontend modules
Module federation or web component composition joins micro frontends at runtime
Each micro frontend has its own build pipeline and deployment
Shell application handles routing and loads micro frontends dynamically
Different micro frontends may use different framework versions

patterns.name: Jamstack
patterns.description: An architecture where the web frontend is pre-rendered at build time and served as static files from a CDN, with dynamic functionality provided by JavaScript calling APIs and serverless functions. Coined by Mathias Biilmann (Netlify). The key insight is that pre-rendering eliminates the server from the request path for most page loads, providing extreme performance and security, while APIs handle dynamic needs.
principles: Pre-render as much as possible at build time
Serve static assets from a CDN for global edge performance
Dynamic functionality via client-side JavaScript calling APIs
Serverless functions handle server-side logic when needed
Git-based workflow with atomic deploys
when_to_use: Content-heavy sites (marketing, documentation, blogs) with mostly static content
Global audience where CDN edge delivery provides significant performance benefit
Security is a priority — no server to attack for pre-rendered pages
Developer experience benefits from decoupled frontend and content management
avoid_when: Highly dynamic content that changes per-request and cannot be pre-rendered
Build time becomes too long when pre-rendering thousands of pages
Real-time interactivity is the primary use case, not content delivery
signals: Static site generator (Next.js, Gatsby, Hugo) pre-renders pages at build time
CDN serves pre-rendered HTML, CSS, and JavaScript assets globally
API calls from client-side JavaScript fetch dynamic data
Serverless functions handle form submissions, authentication, and other server-side logic
Git push triggers build, pre-render, and CDN deployment pipeline

patterns.name: Islands Architecture
patterns.description: A web architecture where the page is primarily static HTML with isolated interactive 'islands' that are independently hydrated on the client side. Described by Katie Sylor-Miller and popularized by Astro framework. The key insight is that most of a typical web page is static content that does not need JavaScript — only the interactive parts (islands) need client-side hydration, dramatically reducing JavaScript payload and improving performance.
principles: Page is mostly static HTML rendered on the server
Interactive components are isolated islands with independent hydration
Each island can use a different framework (React, Vue, Svelte)
Islands hydrate independently — one island loading does not block others
Non-interactive content requires zero client-side JavaScript
when_to_use: Content-heavy pages with sparse interactivity (e.g., a few interactive widgets on a mostly static page)
Performance budget demands minimal JavaScript for page load
Different interactive components on the same page benefit from different frameworks
Progressive enhancement where pages work without JavaScript but enhance with it
avoid_when: Page is highly interactive with most elements requiring client-side state
Islands need to share state extensively, breaking their isolation
Single-page application behavior (client-side routing, persistent state) is required
signals: HTML pages contain isolated interactive components with hydration directives
Framework like Astro, Fresh, or Marko renders pages with island boundaries
JavaScript is loaded only for interactive islands, not for the entire page
Different islands on the same page use different component frameworks
Page functions partially without JavaScript — interactive features enhance progressively

patterns.name: Server Components
patterns.description: A rendering architecture where some UI components execute on the server and stream their rendered output to the client, while other components execute on the client for interactivity. Pioneered by React Server Components. The key insight is that components that only display data can run entirely on the server, eliminating their JavaScript from the client bundle and allowing direct access to server-side data sources without API round-trips.
principles: Server components render on the server and send HTML/payload to the client
Client components handle interactivity and run in the browser
Server components can directly access databases, file systems, and APIs
Server component code is never sent to the client browser
Server and client components compose naturally in the same component tree
when_to_use: Large component trees where most components only display data without interactivity
Need to reduce client-side JavaScript bundle size significantly
Components need direct access to server-side resources (database, file system)
Streaming server rendering can improve time-to-first-byte and progressive content display
avoid_when: Application is highly interactive with most components needing client-side state
Framework does not support server components natively
Server rendering latency is unacceptable for the user experience
signals: Components are explicitly marked as server or client components (e.g., 'use client' directive)
Server components directly query databases without API endpoints
Client bundle size analysis shows server components excluded from the bundle
Streaming response delivers server-rendered content progressively
Component tree mixes server and client components with clear boundaries

patterns.name: Edge Computing Architecture
patterns.description: An architecture where computation is moved to the network edge, closer to end users, rather than running in centralized data centers. Enabled by platforms like Cloudflare Workers, AWS CloudFront Functions, and Deno Deploy. The key insight is that for latency-sensitive operations, moving compute to within milliseconds of the user provides performance improvements that no amount of server optimization in a distant data center can match.
principles: Compute runs at edge locations geographically close to users
Edge functions handle request routing, transformation, and personalization
Heavy computation and stateful operations stay in origin data centers
Edge state is limited — typically cache, KV stores, or small databases
Cold start time is critical as edge functions may start on every request
when_to_use: Latency-sensitive operations where every millisecond of round-trip time matters
Request-level personalization or A/B testing that benefits from edge execution
Geographically distributed users need consistent low-latency responses
Authentication, rate limiting, or header manipulation at the edge reduces origin load
avoid_when: Computation requires access to large datasets that cannot be cached at the edge
Business logic is too complex for the constrained edge runtime environment
Edge runtime limitations (execution time, memory, APIs) are too restrictive
Debugging distributed edge functions is beyond team capability
signals: Functions deployed to edge platforms (Cloudflare Workers, Lambda@Edge, Deno Deploy)
Edge KV stores or caches hold frequently accessed data at edge locations
CDN configuration includes edge compute logic for request/response transformation
Latency metrics show sub-50ms responses from edge locations worldwide
Origin server traffic is reduced because the edge handles many requests directly

patterns.name: Hybrid Cloud Architecture
patterns.description: An architecture where workloads are distributed across on-premises infrastructure and one or more public cloud providers, connected by networking and unified management. The key insight is that not all workloads can or should move to public cloud — regulatory constraints, data gravity, latency requirements, or existing investments create a permanent need for hybrid deployment, which must be a first-class architectural concern.
principles: Workloads are placed on-premises or in cloud based on specific criteria
Unified management layer spans on-premises and cloud environments
Network connectivity between environments is secure and reliable
Data gravity drives placement — data-heavy workloads run near their data
Portable workloads use containers or abstractions for environment mobility
when_to_use: Regulatory requirements mandate certain data or workloads remain on-premises
Existing on-premises investment has not yet been amortized
Burst capacity in cloud supplements steady-state on-premises capacity
Data sovereignty laws require data to remain in specific jurisdictions
Latency-sensitive workloads need proximity to on-premises data sources
avoid_when: Workloads can fully run in public cloud without regulatory or technical constraints
Operational complexity of managing two environments exceeds the benefit
Network connectivity between environments is unreliable or too expensive
signals: Infrastructure spans on-premises data centers and public cloud regions
VPN or dedicated network connections link on-premises to cloud environments
Workload placement policy documents which workloads run where and why
Unified monitoring and management tools cover both on-premises and cloud
Container orchestration (Anthos, Azure Arc, EKS Anywhere) spans environments

patterns.name: Space-Based Architecture
patterns.description: An architecture that achieves high scalability by removing the central database bottleneck, instead distributing both processing and data across multiple processing units with in-memory data grids for state sharing. Named after tuple spaces. The key insight is that the database is typically the scalability bottleneck — by partitioning data in-memory across processing units and using data replication, the architecture can scale horizontally without a central database constraint.
principles: Processing units contain both application logic and in-memory data
Data grid replicates state across processing units
No central database on the hot path — reads and writes go to in-memory state
Data is asynchronously persisted to the database for durability
Messaging grid handles communication between processing units
when_to_use: Application has extreme scalability requirements that database-centric architectures cannot meet
Variable load with dramatic spikes requires elastic scaling of both compute and data
Low-latency reads and writes are critical and database round-trips are unacceptable
Workload is partitionable — different users or sessions can be served by different processing units
avoid_when: Data volume exceeds what can be held in memory across the processing units
Strong consistency across all processing units is a hard requirement
Application is read-heavy with simple queries that a database handles efficiently
signals: In-memory data grid (Hazelcast, Apache Ignite, GemFire) distributes state across nodes
Processing units contain both application code and cached data
Database writes are asynchronous — hot path never waits for database persistence
Data replication between processing units ensures state availability
Processing units can be added or removed dynamically based on load

patterns.name: Blackboard Pattern
patterns.description: An architecture where a shared knowledge base (blackboard) is incrementally built by multiple specialized knowledge sources, each contributing partial solutions when they can. A control component selects which knowledge source acts next. Used in AI, speech recognition, and complex problem-solving systems. The key insight is that ill-structured problems where no single algorithm provides a solution can be tackled by multiple specialized agents contributing partial solutions to a shared workspace.
principles: Shared blackboard holds the current state of the problem solution
Knowledge sources are independent specialists that contribute partial solutions
Control component selects which knowledge source to activate next
Knowledge sources monitor the blackboard and act when they can contribute
Solution emerges incrementally from contributions of multiple knowledge sources
when_to_use: Problem is ill-structured with no single deterministic solution algorithm
Multiple specialized algorithms can contribute partial solutions
Solution requires integration of diverse knowledge from different domains
AI or expert system needs to combine outputs from multiple reasoning engines
avoid_when: Problem has a well-defined algorithmic solution that a single process can compute
Shared state on the blackboard becomes a coordination bottleneck
Knowledge sources have strict ordering requirements that eliminate the benefit of flexibility
signals: Shared data structure holds evolving problem state accessed by multiple components
Multiple specialized processing components read and write to the shared structure
Control loop selects which component acts next based on current state
Solution quality improves incrementally as more components contribute
Components are independent and do not call each other directly

patterns.name: Service Mesh
patterns.description: An infrastructure layer that handles service-to-service communication, providing features like load balancing, mTLS, observability, and traffic management without modifying application code. Implemented by Istio, Linkerd, and Consul Connect. The key insight is that networking concerns (retries, circuit breaking, encryption, tracing) are cross-cutting infrastructure problems that should be solved once in the mesh, not reimplemented in every service.
principles: Data plane proxies intercept all service-to-service traffic
Control plane configures proxy behavior centrally
Application code is unaware of the mesh — communication is transparent
Mutual TLS encrypts all inter-service communication automatically
Traffic management (routing, splitting, mirroring) is configured declaratively
when_to_use: Many microservices need consistent communication policies (retries, timeouts, mTLS)
Observability across services (distributed tracing, metrics) needs to be uniform
Traffic management (canary routing, fault injection) is needed without code changes
Zero-trust security requires mTLS between all services
avoid_when: Fewer than 10 services — mesh overhead exceeds benefit
Team cannot manage the operational complexity of mesh infrastructure
Latency overhead of sidecar proxies is unacceptable for the workload
signals: Sidecar proxy (Envoy) injected into every service pod
Control plane (Istiod, Linkerd control plane) manages mesh configuration
mTLS certificates are automatically rotated for all service-to-service communication
Distributed tracing spans are generated by mesh proxies without application instrumentation
Traffic policies (retry, timeout, circuit breaker) are defined as mesh configuration, not code

patterns.name: Transactional Outbox
patterns.description: A pattern that guarantees reliable event publishing by writing events to an outbox table in the same database transaction as the business data change. A separate process reads the outbox and publishes events to the message broker. The key insight is that dual writes (database + message broker) are inherently unreliable — writing both to the same database transaction eliminates the consistency problem, and a relay process handles publishing.
principles: Events are written to an outbox table in the same transaction as business data
A separate relay process reads the outbox and publishes to the message broker
Outbox entries are marked as published after successful broker delivery
At-least-once delivery is guaranteed — consumers must handle duplicates
No dual write — all changes go to the database first
when_to_use: Service needs to reliably publish events when state changes occur
Dual writes to database and message broker are causing data inconsistency
At-least-once event delivery guarantee is required
Service uses a relational database and a message broker
avoid_when: Event sourcing is used — the event store already serves as the outbox
Message broker supports transactional sends (e.g., Kafka transactions)
Polling overhead of the outbox relay is unacceptable
signals: Outbox table exists in the service database alongside business tables
Business data and outbox entries are written in the same database transaction
Relay process (polling or CDC-based) reads outbox and publishes to message broker
Published flag or deletion marks outbox entries as successfully published
Consumer-side deduplication handles duplicate deliveries

patterns.name: Bulkhead Pattern
patterns.description: A resilience pattern that isolates elements of an application into pools so that if one fails, the others continue to function. Named after ship bulkheads that prevent water from flooding the entire vessel. The key insight is that resource isolation (thread pools, connection pools, process isolation) prevents a failure or resource exhaustion in one component from cascading to others.
principles: Resources are partitioned into isolated pools per component or consumer
Failure in one pool does not exhaust resources available to other pools
Each pool has defined resource limits (threads, connections, memory)
Pools are sized based on expected load and criticality of the component
Monitoring detects pool exhaustion before it impacts other components
when_to_use: Failure in one dependency must not exhaust resources needed by other operations
Different consumers have different SLAs requiring resource isolation
Thread pool or connection pool exhaustion has caused cascading failures in production
Critical and non-critical operations share the same infrastructure
avoid_when: System has few dependencies and isolation overhead is unjustified
Resource utilization is low and pooling would waste reserved capacity
Operational complexity of managing multiple pools exceeds the resilience benefit
signals: Separate thread pools or connection pools exist for different downstream services
Resource limits are configured per dependency or consumer group
Pool exhaustion metrics trigger alerts before cascading failure occurs
Circuit breakers per pool prevent continued requests to failing dependencies
Load shedding activates when a specific pool reaches capacity

patterns.name: Circuit Breaker Pattern
patterns.description: A resilience pattern where a proxy monitors calls to a downstream service and trips to an open state after a threshold of failures, preventing further calls for a cooldown period before allowing tentative retries. Described by Michael Nygard in 'Release It!'. The key insight is that continuing to call a failing service wastes resources and can cause cascading failures — failing fast allows the caller to take alternative action.
principles: Monitor failure rate of calls to downstream services
Trip to open state after failure threshold is exceeded
In open state, fail immediately without making the call
After cooldown, allow a limited number of test calls (half-open state)
Reset to closed state if test calls succeed
when_to_use: Downstream service failures cause timeout-induced resource exhaustion
Caller has fallback behavior when the downstream service is unavailable
Need to prevent cascading failures across service boundaries
Downstream service recovery is aided by reducing load during outage
avoid_when: Operations are fire-and-forget with no need for failure detection
Fallback behavior does not exist and failing fast provides no benefit
Single dependency where circuit breaking just moves the failure earlier
signals: Circuit breaker library (Resilience4j, Polly, Hystrix) wraps downstream calls
Three states visible in metrics: closed (normal), open (failing fast), half-open (testing)
Failure rate thresholds and cooldown periods are configured per downstream service
Fallback logic executes when circuit is open
Dashboard shows circuit breaker state transitions and trip frequency

patterns.name: Domain-Driven Design (DDD)
patterns.description: An approach to software development that centers the design on the core business domain, using a ubiquitous language shared between developers and domain experts. Introduced by Eric Evans in 2003. The key insight is that the most significant complexity in software is the domain itself — aligning code structure with domain concepts reduces translation overhead and enables the model to evolve with the business.
principles: Ubiquitous language bridges developers and domain experts
Bounded contexts define explicit boundaries for domain models
Aggregates enforce consistency boundaries within a bounded context
Domain events capture significant business occurrences
Context mapping defines relationships between bounded contexts
Strategic design guides decomposition into bounded contexts
when_to_use: Business domain is complex with rich rules, invariants, and processes
Collaboration between developers and domain experts is feasible and valuable
Multiple teams need to work on different parts of a complex domain
Domain model needs to evolve with changing business requirements
avoid_when: Domain is simple CRUD with no significant business rules
Domain experts are not available for collaboration
Project is a short-lived prototype where domain modeling overhead is unjustified
signals: Code uses domain-specific terminology that matches business language
Bounded context boundaries align with team and service boundaries
Aggregate roots enforce consistency for groups of related entities
Context map documents relationships (shared kernel, customer-supplier, etc.) between contexts
Domain events are explicitly modeled and published when significant state changes occur
Value objects and entities are used instead of primitive types for domain concepts

patterns.name: API Gateway Pattern
patterns.description: A pattern where a single entry point handles all client requests, routing them to appropriate backend services while providing cross-cutting concerns like authentication, rate limiting, and request transformation. The key insight is that clients should not need to know the internal decomposition of the backend — the gateway provides a unified, stable API surface while the backend services evolve independently.
principles: Single entry point for all client requests
Request routing to appropriate backend services
Cross-cutting concerns (auth, rate limiting, logging) applied uniformly
Response aggregation combines data from multiple services
API versioning and backward compatibility managed at the gateway
when_to_use: Microservices backend needs a unified API surface for clients
Cross-cutting concerns must be applied consistently across all services
Client needs aggregated responses from multiple backend services
API versioning and deprecation must be managed centrally
avoid_when: Single backend service — gateway adds unnecessary latency
Gateway becomes a bottleneck or single point of failure
Gateway logic grows into a monolithic orchestration layer
signals: API gateway (Kong, AWS API Gateway, Envoy) sits in front of all backend services
Gateway configuration maps external URLs to internal service endpoints
Authentication and rate limiting are enforced at the gateway, not in each service
Response transformation reshapes backend responses for client consumption
Gateway metrics show request routing, latency, and error rates per route

patterns.name: Event Mesh
patterns.description: A dynamic infrastructure layer that routes events between decoupled applications, cloud services, and devices regardless of where they are deployed. Unlike a single event broker, an event mesh connects multiple brokers across environments into a unified event routing fabric. The key insight is that as event-driven architectures span multiple clouds, regions, and environments, a single broker is insufficient — an interconnected mesh of brokers provides global event routing with local performance.
principles: Events are routed dynamically across interconnected brokers
Producers and consumers are location-agnostic
Topic-based or content-based routing determines event destinations
Mesh spans on-premises, cloud, and edge environments
Events are delivered with defined quality of service guarantees
when_to_use: Event-driven architecture spans multiple clouds or data centers
Events need to flow between on-premises and cloud environments
Dynamic event routing based on content or context is required
Single event broker cannot meet geographic distribution requirements
avoid_when: All services run in a single cloud region and a single broker suffices
Event volume is low enough that direct broker connections handle the load
Operational complexity of managing a mesh of brokers exceeds the benefit
signals: Multiple event brokers are interconnected across environments
Event routing rules dynamically direct events based on topic or content
Events flow between cloud regions, data centers, or edge locations transparently
Event mesh management console shows topology and routing configuration
Quality of service settings (delivery guarantee, ordering) are configured per event type

patterns.name: Data Lake Architecture
patterns.description: An architecture where raw data from diverse sources is stored in its native format in a centralized repository, enabling schema-on-read rather than schema-on-write. Coined by James Dixon of Pentaho. The key insight is that deferring schema definition to query time preserves optionality — data scientists and analysts can apply different schemas to the same raw data for different analytical purposes.
principles: Store raw data in its native format without upfront transformation
Schema-on-read: structure is applied when data is queried, not when stored
Single repository for structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data
Data governance prevents the lake from becoming a data swamp
Metadata catalog enables discovery and understanding of stored data
when_to_use: Need to store diverse data types (logs, images, JSON, CSV) in one repository
Analytical use cases are not known at data ingestion time
Data scientists need access to raw data for exploration and model training
Cost-effective storage of large volumes of data for future analysis
avoid_when: All queries need structured, well-defined schemas — a data warehouse is better
Data governance is immature and the lake will become an ungoverned swamp
Data volume is small enough that a traditional database handles all needs
signals: Object storage (S3, ADLS, GCS) serves as the primary data repository
Data is stored in raw format (Parquet, Avro, JSON, CSV) without transformation
Metadata catalog (Glue, Hive Metastore, Unity Catalog) indexes stored data
Query engines (Athena, Presto, Spark SQL) apply schema at query time
Data zones (raw, curated, enriched) organize data by processing stage
